0: AGRICULTURAL ISSUES STRATEGIES FOR THE FUTURE
Balancing Production Output and Environmental Impact in Animal Production
Adrian G. Williams and Eric Audsley Natural Resource Management Centre, Cranfield University, Bedford, MK43 OAL, UK. adrian.williams@cranfield.ac.uk www.agrilca.org
1: <header>1 Introduction </header>

Animal production supplies consumers with high quality protein in meat, eggs and milk together with other nutrients, like fat and minerals, as well as wool and leather. The conversion of animal feed into useful outputs generates waste products that have substantial impacts on the environment, e.g. methane, ammonia and nitrous oxide. In addition, the production of feeds consumes resources, such as direct energy for cultivation together with indirect energy embodied in materials like fertilisers and equipment like tractors or milking machines. It also leads to diffuse pollution from, for example, nitrate, phosphate and nitrous oxide.

Agriculture contributes to about 8% of UK greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (Garnett, 2008) and livestock production worldwide has been estimated to contribute to 18% of the world GHG emissions (FAO, 2006), although this figure has been questioned.

Unabsorbed N emerges in excreta and is a major source of ammonia emissions. About 260 kt NH3 were emitted by agriculture in 2006 of which 88% came from livestock and 12% from fertiliser application, some of which would be for livestock feed. Animal production contributed 73% to the UK total national emissions of 315 kt NH3 in 2006. Under the 1999 Gothenburg Protocol to Abate Acidification, Eutrophication and Ground-level Ozone, we are obliged to reduce the ammonia emission levels in 2010 by 17% compared to 1990 NAEI (2008).

Various environmental assessment methods have been developed and applied to livestock production systems (Halberg et al., 2005a, Lilywhite et al., 2007), e.g. ecological, environmental and carbon footprinting, nutrient surpluses per ha, specific gaseous emissions and environmental Life Cycle Assessment (LCA). Most differ in their underlying assumptions, system and geographical boundaries and their purpose, e.g. reducing nitrate leaching per ha or improving input-output efficiencies. LCA is the most holistic methodology available that considers the environmental burdens and resource use in the production of a commodity from 'cradle to grave' or 'field to fork' and output based methods were favoured by Halberg et al. (2005a) for comparing and improving production systems. The calculations required (and hence outputs) can also be applied in some other methods. LCA thus overcomes many of the limitations of the other assessment methods. LCA considers all the activities involved in producing a useful output the Functional Unit (FU), which typically a quantity, sometimes further defined by location and time, e.g. 1 m³ fat corrected milk at the farm gate.

A team at Cranfield University (initially Silsoe Research Institute) has applied LCA to agricultural production in the UK using system modelling, coupled with LCA (Williams, et al., 2006, Williams et al., 2007). Some the approaches and findings are presented here. A more recent project for Defra has

compared lamb produced in New Zealand and beef in Brazil with domestic production, but the final report has not been issued yet (details should become available at http://www.defra.gov.uk/Science/ and searching for project FO0103).

5: <header>2 The LCA Approach </header>

LCA traces the production of any defined amount of a product back to natural resources in the ground (e.g. crude oil and rock phosphate) and calculates the emissions to the environment from all processes, leading to the final product as it emerges from a pre-defined system boundary (Figure 1), e.g. the farm gate or retail distribution centre ("cradle to gate" boundaries).

Our work has concentrated on the cradle to (farm) gate aspects and has mainly concerned producing the life cycle inventories (LCIs) for each commodity studied. LCIs contain potentially many items that characterise an inventory. On the resource use side, these include non-renewable energy and abiotic resources (non-biological, non-renewable, e.g. natural gas, lime, iron ore) and all can be aggregated into one weighted value for abiotic resource use (ABU). This relates the use of resource to its total estimated global reserves and quantifies it as the equivalent use of antimony (University of Leiden, 2008).

On the emissions side, GHG are calculated separately and aggregated into aggregated Global Warming Potentials (GWP), Table 1. In agriculture and its supply chain, the main ones are CO2 from fossil fuel, CH4 from enteric emissions, manure management and N2O from N turnover in the soil, fertiliser production and manure management. Downstream as agricultural outputs progress through the supply chain, fossil CO2 (and sometimes refrigerants) become more important. A further flux of CO2 is between soil carbon and the atmosphere, which can be positive or negative.

The consumption of resources and emissions to the environmental are collectively termed environmental burdens. All material flows must be measured or calculated into and out of the system boundary. For crops, environmental emissions, like nitrate leaching, are calculated per unit area, but total nitrate leaching per ha is related through net yield to a tonne of crop. The emissions that contributed to particular environmental damage are aggregated into potential values for causing that harm. The main potentials of concern are global warming, eutrophication (mainly nitrate, phosphate and ammonia), acidification (sulphur dioxide and ammonia) and ozone depletion (mainly refrigerants). Weighting factors for eutrophication, acidification and ozone depletion come from the University of Leiden (2008), which those for global warming come from the IPCC (2006).

8: It is unusual in any agricultural system to produce only the intended commodity; it is much more likely that a waste product, by-product or co-product will ensue. The choice of category for the output is sometimes difficult to pin down, but things without further value are clearly wastes and things with some values are usually co-products. Straw is a co-product from wheat production and manure is a co-product of animal production. The (sometimes very challenging) question for LCA practitioners is how to allocate burdens (e.g. energy use or eutrophication) between co-products. Various methods are applied to try and avoid this if possible but functional approaches are preferred where possible. Where outputs are disparate, the only practical solution is to use an economic valuation to apportion burdens.
9: <header>2.2 Crop production </header>

This is where all animal feeds start and crop production forms a main part of the burdens of animal production. We modelled both non-organic and organic crop production in typical rotations. This was done in order to establish balanced inputs and outputs of the main plant nutrients: NPK. N is the most complex nutrient as it can be lost in several ways and gets buffered by a large organic N pool in the soil. The SUNDIAL crop-soil simulation model from Rothamsted was used to calculate the long term leaching losses from crops. We also used the results of long term experiments from Rothamsted's Broadbalk plots to create true long term yield response curves to applied N. Rotations were modelled with a combination of three levels of rainfall and soil texture from which a national representative set could be derived.
For P and K, the long term mass balances can be derived more simply as being
inputs = Yofftake + Closses
Inputs are typically from applied fertiliser, manure and atmospheric deposition. Offtake depends on yield and crop composition (allowing for the fate of straw) plus diffuse losses.

Crop production also requires direct energy for cultivation and harvesting plus cooling and drying. Cultivation energy varies with soil texture, with sandy soils needing only about 40% of the energy for plough based cultivation as clay soils. Harvesting and surface based activities, like spraying, are effectively independent of soil type. Heavier soils do, however support high yields for many crops.

Crops also require indirect energy for the manufacture of fertiliser, pesticides and machinery. In non- organic cereal production, the largest single term is usually N fertiliser. In organic crops, it may be machinery, which is relatively high owing to lower yields and the absence of synthesised N.

Much of our work has been to derive LCI for inputs, like fertilisers, pesticides and machinery (Williams, et al., 2006, Audsley et al, 1997). The application of systems modelling also ensure that all activities and linked and that "cheating" is not allowed. For example, in a long term context offtake must be supported by the nutrient supply, using reduced cultivation techniques takes less energy per ha, but requires more herbicide use.
Much feed is based on domestic production, but some crops are imported from overseas. We included two main ones: maize from the USA and soya from the Americas.

12: Grass is a special crop, and one that we grow well in the UK. It grows across a wide range of soil types and locations SO that altitude must be taken into account as well as rainfall and soil texture. A grass production model was thus developed to account for these geographic factors as well as the N supply (e.g. from clover or synthetic N). The type of animal grazing also affects grass growth, because diary cattle retain more ingested N than beef cattle or sheep, SO that the proportion that is returned in excreta varies. The excretory N stimulates grass growth, but some is also lost as NH3, NO3 and N2O. The model thus calculates the dry matter yield for pasture types, together with emissions to the environment plus the agronomic needs of fertilisation and field work.
13: Crop processing varies from crushing home grown cereals on the farm to extracting oil from crops like soya and rape in standalone operations or feed mills. In feed mills, energy is used for processes like blending, grinding, steaming and pelleting. Additional energy and resources may also be used for packaging and subsequent distribution. There are major interactions with allied industries, such as four milling from which major co-products, like wheatfeed, derived.

Generic energy consumption was obtained from the industry and combined with specific processes for partitioning crops like, wheat, maize, soya and rape. In these cases, economic allocation was applied to parturition the burdens between primary and secondary components.
Where we did not have specific LCI data for crops or by-products that are used in feeds, we used the nearest equivalent as surrogates. Minerals were collectively equated to rock phosphate.

Generic feed mixes were produced for all major livestock classes based on a combination of publicly available (but rather old) data from Defra and some more recent data from the industry. Diets were designed to meet nutritional needs.
14: We have two approaches to this. For pigs and poultry, production systems were modelled in the basis of reported values for feed input and daily gain, feed conversion ratio etc for different ages and types of stock. The situation for grazing stock is more interesting and complex. A common criterion for sheep and cattle is to meet energy needs. Most of these for sheep are met by grazing, with some winter forage and concentrates. The dominant grazing energy is met by grass dry matter, the supply of which is calculated from the grass model. This in turn gives the land requirement for grazing sheep.

In cattle, energy and protein needs are calculated according to maintenance needs and outputs, such as milk yield and daily liveweight gain. The proportion of nutrients coming from grazing or conserved forage are defined, SO that the balance is met by concentrates, which are tailored to the type of stock. The proportion of forage in the diets is used to scale enteric methane emissions.
In dairy cattle, the energy and protein needs are dominated by milk yield and composition, together with maintenance, which is a function of liveweight.
15: The structures of national flocks and herds were modelled SO that interactions could be understood. For poultry and pigs, these are relatively simple pyramids, with breeders at the top. Sheep are a complete contrast with stratification by altitude and interactions between purebred and crossbred types to make maximum use of resources and maximise hybrid vigour (Figure 2).

16: <header>Figure 2 Structure of the national sheep flock (simplified) </header>

The structure is modelled with a set of linear equations that describe all of the inputs and outputs of each "box", together with linkages between them. The equations can be solved to establish what the industry structure must be like in order to deliver the functional unit of 1 t lamb carcass, given specified constraints. This in turn allows different configurations of the industry to be explored.
17: Manure is inevitable in animal production. It is a waste product as far as animals are concerned, but does contain plant nutrients and organic carbon that can help improve soil structure. Manure also, unfortunately, is an emitter of pollutants, especially from unabsorbed N, together with methane. Manure is managed in order to obtain something useful from it, which implies the consumption of energy. All this is handled with a manure model. The nutrient composition is calculated from a mass balance of feed inputs and offtake. Losses in gases or liquids are then accounted for at every stage from housing (or grazing), through storage to land spreading and these are coupled with the energy and machinery needs at each stage. The benefit of remaining plant nutrients is then quantified by calculating the yield response to the N in manure (on grass or wheat). The yield response is equated to either a synthetic N fertiliser for non-organic systems or a sacrificial legume for organic. P and K are assumed to be fully used on a long term basis.
The net result is that the animals are credited with the benefits of plant nutrients but are debited with costs of manure management. Manure also acts as a way of linking crop and animal production.

The yield response is equated to either a synthetic N fertiliser for non-organic systems or a sacrificial legume for organic. P and K are assumed to be fully used on a long term basis.
The net result is that the animals are credited with the benefits of plant nutrients but are debited with costs of manure management. Manure also acts as a way of linking crop and animal production.
19: The structures of the sectors are linked with systems models that draw components together and contain the equations that define the need for feeds and other inputs. These also contain relationships or constants to describe factors like longevity, mortality etc. Apart from feed, the other inputs are housing and direct energy (e.g. heating or ventilation plus vehicles for outdoor stockmanship). Manure is also accounted for.
The feed requirements are linked to LCI data for the individual feed crops, together with processing and delivery.

The functional unit for each meat output is 1 t edible carcass weight at the farm gate. This is liveweight X killing out percentage, SO this must be known, but the burdens of transport to the slaughterhouse and slaughtering are not included in our analysis. Packaging is not included with eggs.

The life cycle inventories for some feed unprocessed crops (Table 2) show that energy use is in the same order of magnitude for each crop, but that the use of energy is contrasted between non-legumes and legumes. Although feed what uses much more energy per ha, the energy use per t is still similar to beans owing to the higher yield of wheat. Oilseed rape stands out as being the highest energy user, but the crop properties are quite different from cereals or pulses, with much higher energy nutritional per unit crop weight than the others, because of its oil content. Once feed crops have been processed, other factors are introduced, like delivery transport, and particularly, allocation between primary and secondary streams (e.g. milling wheat flour and wheatfeed or rape oil and meal). contingent on the by-product being available, SO that a massive move away from white flour would reduce the supply of wheatfeed, increase its value and hence its burdens. While LCA is not designed to be an economic analysis tool, it does respond to some market forces.

Grazing is a relatively low-energy source of dry matter (Table 4) and the production energy tends to decrease with altitude. Ensilaging clearly requires more mechanical effort and hence more than doubles the energy needed.

The life cycle inventories for some feed unprocessed crops (Table 2) show that energy use is in the same order of magnitude for each crop, but that the use of energy is contrasted between non-legumes and legumes. N fertiliser dominates non-legumes and field work dominates legumes. Although feed what uses much more energy per ha, the energy use per t is still similar to beans owing to the higher yield of wheat. Oilseed rape stands out as being the highest energy user, but the crop properties are quite different from cereals or pulses, with much higher energy nutritional per unit crop weight than the others, because of its oil content. It is of course grown because of this.

Once feed crops have been processed, other factors are introduced, like delivery transport, and particularly, allocation between primary and secondary streams (e.g. milling wheat flour and wheatfeed or rape oil and meal). This results in much lower burdens for wheatfeed than other crops or processed crops (Table 3). This highlights the fact that using by-products is a good thing. It is

contingent on the by-product being available, SO that a massive move away from white flour would reduce the supply of wheatfeed, increase its value and hence its burdens. While LCA is not designed to be an economic analysis tool, it does respond to some market forces.

Grazing is a relatively low-energy source of dry matter (Table 4) and the production energy tends to decrease with altitude. Ensilaging clearly requires more mechanical effort and hence more than doubles the energy needed.

8: The burdens of the national average animal production outputs show some important features (Table 6). Allowing for the radically different nutritional properties of crops and meats, it is apparent that it takes much more to produce 1 t fresh weight meat than crops, with energy use being about an order of magnitude higher that for crops. This is not surprising, because animals concentrate crops into high quality protein as well as fat and other nutrients. What is more apparent is that the effects on eutrophication and acidification are about two orders of magnitude more that in crops. Much of this is attributable to unabsorbed protein being emitted to the environment as ammonia (via urea and uric
acid). Ammonia contributes to acidification, because it enters the atmosphere as a weak alkali, but when deposited on soil, it gats oxidised by nitrifying soil bacteria to nitric acid.

There is also an interesting contrast between the relatively low energy-consuming commodities of poultry meat and lamb. The GWP per unit of lamb is about five times that of poultry meat. Much of this is attributable to enteric methane as well as nitrous oxide from grazing (Table 7). In poultry meat production, over 85% of GWP is from feed production, whereas it is about 60% for lamb. The proportion of energy used feed production dominates, ranging from 70% (milk and pig meat) to 88% (beef and lamb).

The relatively high direct energy by poultry meat and pig production arises from heating young stock, ventilating buildings while milk cooling contributes a large amount to milk production. Manure presents interesting contrasts with negative energy use and positive GWP, resulting from the substitution of manure for fertiliser. The magnitude of the energy credit for manure is a mixture of the relatively high nutrient content of manure balanced against the energy costs of management, SO poultry manure scores highly owing its high nutrient and dry matter concentration. While manure has benefits, it also reflects inefficiency in that the plant nutrients have not been absorbed by the livestock.

The huge dominance of feed in both energy use and environmental emissions clearly indicate that making good use of feed is critical in making animal production more environmentally efficient, as well as helping profitability. Genetic improvement has been estimated to make decreases in GHG and ammonia from layer hens, broiler chickens, dairy cow and pig production of about 1% per year over the last 20 years (Jones et al., 2008). Other improvements related to better nutrition and husbandry (e.g. reduced morbidity and mortality).

acid). Ammonia contributes to acidification, because it enters the atmosphere as a weak alkali, but when deposited on soil, it gats oxidised by nitrifying soil bacteria to nitric acid.

The huge dominance of feed in both energy use and environmental emissions clearly indicate that making good use of feed is critical in making animal production more environmentally efficient, as well as helping profitability. Genetic improvement has been estimated to make decreases in GHG and ammonia from layer hens, broiler chickens, dairy cow and pig production of about 1% per year over the last 20 years (Jones et al., 2008). Other improvements related to better nutrition and husbandry (e.g. reduced morbidity and mortality).

Allowing for the radically different nutritional properties of crops and meats, it is apparent that it takes much more to produce 1 t fresh weight meat than crops, with energy use being about an order of magnitude higher that for crops. This is not surprising, because animals concentrate crops into high quality protein as well as fat and other nutrients. What is more apparent is that the effects on eutrophication and acidification are about two orders of magnitude more that in crops. 13: We modelled the national dairy herd with three levels of production, each with defined characteristics (Table 9). As yield increases, COWS become heavier, SO increasing absolute maintenance requirement per cow, but still decreasing the proportion of burdens attributable to milk. If a smaller cow could produce as much milk as the "standard" large cow, the improvements continue, but more for GWP than energy. This is because the smaller cow must still meet its energy needs and being smaller, it needs a higher proportion of concentrates to achieve this. Concentrates take more energy per unit weight, but a higher inclusion rate reduces GHG emissions. Were it possible to increase feed conversion efficiency (for milk synthesis only) by 10%, then energy use would fall by a further 12%

Doubling or halving the proportion of lowland ewes in the national sheep flock causes the whole flock to be restructured. The outcomes (Table 10) show that moving towards more lowland production increases resource use, but decreases the GWP, which constitutes the "carbon footprint". In most of non-agricultural industry, energy and carbon footprint and very closely linked.

16: A life cycle approach to animal production yield valuable insights into animal production. Most resources used and a large promotion of emissions to the environment are associated with feed production, SO highlighting the great importance of understanding (and improving) feed production and utilisation by livestock. There are apparent systematic differences between ruminant and non- ruminant production that are associated with enteric methane emissions. Ruminants possess the marvellous capacity to use forages and various by-products that are of little value to non-ruminants. Increasing global warming through methane emissions is the consequence. Finding robust solutions to this that do not otherwise increase environmental burdens unacceptably is a key challenge for animal science. Livestock production is often highly constrained and improvements in environmental performance must be examined in a systems framework to ensure that interactions are adequately explored and accounted for.

The use of by-products is good practice, I that they incur relative small resource use and as long as they are available. Unbalanced use can, of course, lead to more N emissions and adverse environmental effects as a consequence.
